title: 💰 10 Deductions 90% of Freelancers Forget Each Year
description:
Are you self-employed in the US or UK and paying more tax than you should? In this video I walk through ten deductions most freelancers miss, with citations and real numbers. Schedule C tips, Self-Employment tax, and write-offs that actually move the needle in 2026.

Personal finance, freelancer taxes, Schedule C deductions, US and UK tax savings.

segments.voice: The average self-employed worker in the United States overpays income tax by about two thousand to four thousand dollars a year. Not because the IRS is wrong, but because the freelancer never claimed deductions they were entitled to. According to a two thousand twenty-four NASE survey, more than ninety percent of solo earners leave write-offs on the table. Today you stop being one of them.
segments.visual: person frustrated looking at tax documents calculator

segments.voice: Before we hit the deductions, one rule sets the foundation. For an expense to be deductible on Schedule C, the IRS requires three things, spelled out in Publication 535. First, the cost has to be ordinary and necessary for your trade. Second, you need a paper trail, a receipt, an invoice, or a bank record. Third, the amount has to be reasonable for the work you do.
segments.visual: legal documents tax law books desk

segments.voice: Miss any of those three pieces and an auditor can kick the deduction out years later, with penalties on top. A photo of a coffee receipt is not a substitute for a real invoice with your name and the vendor's tax ID. UK readers, HMRC asks for the same paper trail when you file your Self Assessment. Quarterly estimated tax filers, the Form 1040-ES math is only as good as the records behind it.
segments.visual: invoice receipt documents organized folder

segments.voice: Deduction number one, the home office. If you work from a dedicated space at home, IRS Publication 587 lets you deduct a percentage of utilities, internet, rent, and mortgage interest. The simple method is five dollars per square foot, capped at three hundred square feet, so fifteen hundred a year with no extra paperwork. The actual expense method takes more work but usually pays double or triple.
segments.visual: home office workspace electricity bills

segments.voice: Here is a real example. Your apartment is one thousand square feet and your office takes two hundred. That is twenty percent. If your utilities and internet run two hundred dollars a month, twenty percent of that, forty bucks, is deductible. Stretch that across twelve months and you have four hundred and eighty dollars off your taxable income just for the corner you already use to work.
segments.visual: calculator home bills apartment floor plan

segments.voice: Deduction two, your phone bill. If you use your cell for client calls, write off the business percentage. The IRS does not give a fixed percentage, you have to justify it. Cleanest approach is a second SIM or a virtual line. If you only have one phone, a written log for one typical month sets the percentage you apply for the year.
segments.visual: smartphone business calls office professional

segments.voice: Most CPAs comfortable with one-line freelancers will defend a fifty percent business use claim, sometimes seventy. Just be ready to back it up if the IRS asks. With the average US wireless bill sitting around eighty-five dollars a month per Consumer Reports two thousand twenty-five, fifty percent is over five hundred dollars a year staying in your pocket. Small habit, real money.
segments.visual: person talking phone taking notes office

segments.voice: Deduction three, your vehicle. This one trips people up. The standard mileage rate for two thousand twenty-six is sixty-seven cents per business mile, set by the IRS each January. The actual expense method lets you deduct gas, insurance, repairs, depreciation. You pick one method the first year, and you cannot keep flipping between them on a leased car.
segments.visual: car keys documents vehicle registration

segments.voice: If you do field work, sales, deliveries, or client visits, the mileage stacks up fast. A real estate agent driving fifteen thousand business miles a year claims roughly ten thousand dollars in deduction at the sixty-seven cent rate. The catch, you need a contemporaneous mileage log. Apps like MileIQ or Stride keep it clean. No log, the IRS will throw the whole thing out in an audit.
segments.visual: gas station fuel receipt highway driving

segments.voice: Deduction four, business meals. Per IRS Publication 463, fifty percent of meals with clients or while traveling overnight for work is deductible. The food has to be ordinary, not lavish, and you need to note who was there and what was discussed on the receipt. The hundred percent meal deduction from twenty twenty-one and twenty twenty-two is gone, so do not let an old blog post mislead you.
segments.visual: business lunch restaurant receipt meal

segments.voice: Pay with a card, never cash, and grab the itemized receipt, not just the credit slip. If you eat with a client twice a week at fifty dollars a head, that is roughly twenty-six hundred dollars in deductible expense and a real tax saving in the four hundred dollar range for most filers. Track it as you go, not in April when memory is gone.
segments.visual: credit card payment restaurant terminal

segments.voice: Deduction five, professional development. Courses, conferences, books, and online subscriptions tied to your work are fully deductible per Publication 535. That covers Coursera, Udemy, industry conferences, and yes, the books on your shelf you bought for actual reference. UK freelancers, HMRC has the same rule for training that maintains existing skills, though new-skill training is trickier.
segments.visual: online course laptop learning professional training

segments.voice: Here is something most people miss. Software you use for work, your Adobe Creative Cloud, your Notion, your Figma, your accounting tools like QuickBooks or Wave, all deductible. Even the AI subscription you use to draft client emails. Save the email receipts in a folder so you can reconcile them at the end of the year without digging through inboxes.
segments.visual: books stack professional development certificates

segments.voice: Deduction six, business insurance. Liability insurance, errors and omissions, cyber liability, all deductible on Schedule C line fifteen. Health insurance premiums for a self-employed person come out as an above-the-line deduction on Schedule 1, not Schedule C, but they still reduce taxable income. The Self-Employed Health Insurance deduction is in IRC section 162(l).
segments.visual: insurance policy documents health coverage papers

segments.voice: Crunch the numbers. A freelancer paying eight hundred dollars a month for a marketplace health plan moves nine thousand six hundred dollars from taxable to non-taxable income. At a twenty-two percent marginal bracket, that is twenty-one hundred dollars saved in federal tax alone, before state. Add liability cover at fifty dollars a month and the math keeps stacking.
segments.visual: family health insurance happy family doctor

segments.voice: Deduction seven, dues and subscriptions. Professional memberships, trade groups, and journals tied to your work go on Schedule C. American Bar Association dues for a lawyer, an Institute of Management Accountants membership for a CPA, a SAG-AFTRA membership for an actor. The hobbyist version of these does not count. Direct ties to your trade is the test the IRS applies.
segments.visual: professional association membership card certificate

segments.voice: Deduction eight, depreciation. When you buy equipment over a few hundred dollars, like a laptop, camera, or office furniture, you usually spread the deduction across its useful life under MACRS rules in IRS Publication 946. A computer is five years, office furniture is seven. There is a Section 179 election that lets you write off most equipment in year one, capped at one million two hundred thousand dollars for two thousand twenty-six.
segments.visual: computer equipment office setup new laptop

segments.voice: Here is the move pros use. If you have a high-income year, take Section 179 immediately and crush your tax bill. If your income is low, spread the depreciation to offset a future high-earning year. Talk this through with a CPA before you swipe the card on a fifteen hundred dollar laptop in late December. Timing the purchase changes the whole picture.
segments.visual: financial spreadsheet depreciation calculation desk

segments.voice: Deduction nine, business interest and bank fees. Interest on a business credit card or loan, bank fees on your business checking, and merchant processing fees from Stripe, Square, or PayPal all flow into Schedule C. The Bureau of Labor Statistics tracks small business banking costs averaging two hundred forty dollars a year, low six figures over a career if you never write them off.
segments.visual: bank loan documents interest rates finance

segments.voice: Most freelancers run everything through one personal account. That is the single biggest organizational mistake I see. Open a separate business checking, route all client payments and business spending through it, and your bookkeeping problem solves itself. Banks like Mercury, Novo, and Bluevine offer no-fee business accounts with no minimums. There is no good reason to mix the two anymore.
segments.visual: bank account statements separate accounts organized

segments.voice: Deduction ten, the qualified business income deduction. This is the big one most people skip. Per IRC section 199A, most self-employed filers can deduct up to twenty percent of qualified business income on Form 8995. Phase-outs kick in around two hundred thirty-two thousand dollars single, four hundred sixty-four thousand married for two thousand twenty-six, but most freelancers fall well below that.
segments.visual: percentage calculation tax savings money

segments.voice: An example. Your net profit on Schedule C is sixty thousand dollars. Twenty percent of that is twelve thousand dollars knocked off your taxable income before you even hit a tax bracket. At a twenty-two percent bracket, that is twenty-six hundred dollars in real tax savings. The QBI deduction does not reduce self-employment tax, only income tax, so do not confuse the two when you forecast.
segments.visual: tax form calculation simplified deduction

segments.voice: Pulling it together. A freelancer earning sixty thousand who claims home office, mileage, health insurance, and QBI properly can drop their taxable income by fifteen to twenty thousand. At combined federal, state, and self-employment rates around thirty-five percent, that is five to seven thousand dollars staying in their account every year. Compound that over a decade and we are talking real wealth.
segments.visual: money savings piggy bank dollars growing

segments.voice: One last move that pays off. Review your last three returns. The IRS lets you amend with Form 1040-X within three years of filing if you missed deductions. Yes, you can get refunds for prior years, with interest. UK filers, you have four years from the end of the tax year to amend a Self Assessment under HMRC overpayment relief rules. Money is sitting there if you forgot to claim it.
segments.visual: calendar tax deadline documents review office

segments.voice: Subscribe to FinanzasClara and hit the bell. What's coming next week, the freelancer mistake that cost one Reddit user eleven thousand dollars in a single audit, plus the one piece of paper that would have saved him. Drop a comment with the deduction you want me to break down next, and I will pin the best ones at the top.
segments.visual: subscribe button notification bell youtube channel
